title: Open Source & Inner Source Strategy
description: Open source and inner source strategies for SAP engineers transitioning to FAANG. Covers why open source matters at FAANG, FAANG's inner source practices (open by default, internal forks), the culture shift from SAP's closed/patented ecosystem, OSS licensing basics, and how to start contributing to open source as an SAP engineer with no prior OSS experience.

sections.title: From SAP's Closed Ecosystem to FAANG's Open Culture

description.value:
SAP's engineering culture is built on proprietary code, closed development, partner licensing, and IP protection above all else. Every line of ABAP is locked inside a system behind a client login. Source code is visible only to the specific customer or partner instance. Cross-system visibility requires explicit transport authorization. The core premise of SAP development is: this code is a trade secret and must never be visible outside this system.

FAANG operates on the exact opposite premise. At Google, the entire internal monorepo is visible to every engineer in the company — over 50,000 engineers can read any line of code. At Meta, internal code is open by default across the entire organization. At Amazon, most infrastructure teams maintain their projects in repositories visible to all internal engineers. The default assumption is transparency, not secrecy. Code is closed only when there is an explicit security or competitive reason to keep it private.

The culture shock is immense for SAP engineers. At FAANG, your code will be read, reviewed, and criticized by thousands of engineers you have never met. Every API you design must be clean enough to be consumed by anyone in the company. Every abstraction you create must be documented well enough for an engineer in a different time zone to use it without asking you. The SAP mindset of 'this is my custom program for my client' does not survive at FAANG — you must adopt the mindset of 'I am building a platform for thousands of internal customers.'

Inner source is the application of open source practices — pull requests, code review, community contribution, maintainer roles — to code that remains within a company's firewall. Every FAANG company has adopted inner source as its default development model. The key practices are:

Open by default. All new repositories start as visible to all internal engineers. You need an explicit justification to make a repository private. This is the direct opposite of SAP, where every object is locked to a specific package and transport layer.

Internal forks. If you need to modify code owned by another team, you fork their repository, make changes, and submit a pull request back to the original team. This is the direct analogy to SAP's cross-project structure — but without transport restrictions, without abapGit merge conflicts, without the need for a Basis team to approve your transport. The friction is dramatically lower.

Contribution culture. At FAANG, any engineer can send a pull request to any team's repository. The owning team is expected to review and respond within a defined SLA (typically 24-48 hours). This creates a culture where engineers naturally contribute across team boundaries. At SAP, modifying another team's code requires a formal change request process, transport coordination, and often a separate change advisory board (CAB) approval. At FAANG, you just open a PR.

Implication for SAP engineers: Your experience navigating SAP's complex cross-module dependencies (FI to SD to MM to WM) is directly relevant to inner source. You already understand the complexity of enterprise integrations. The difference is that at FAANG, you can actually submit the fix yourself — you don't need to file a ticket with the owning team and wait 3 sprints.

sections.title: Why FAANG Invests in Open Source

description.value:
FAANG companies are not charitable organizations — they invest in open source because it creates strategic business advantage. Understanding why FAANG invests in OSS is essential for SAP engineers to understand where their contributions will be valued.

Community contributions. When Google open-sourced Kubernetes, thousands of external contributors built features, fixed bugs, and documented the platform. Google received free engineering labor from the entire world. SAP could never match this scale of engineering investment with internal teams alone. Kubernetes is now the standard for container orchestration because of this community contribution flywheel, not because Google built all of it internally.

Industry standard setting. TensorFlow (Google), PyTorch (Meta), React (Meta), Presto (Facebook/Meta), and Apache Spark (originated at UC Berkeley, heavily invested in by FAANG) became industry standards because they were open source. When an SAP technology (like CDS or RAP) is proprietary, only SAP customers use it. When a FAANG technology is open source, every competitor, startup, and cloud provider must integrate with it. This is a powerful moat.

Talent attraction. Open source projects serve as a recruiting funnel. Engineers want to work on technologies they already use and love. SAP's proprietary stack means SAP must recruit engineers who are willing to learn its ecosystem from scratch. FAANG recruits engineers who already contribute to their open source projects. This is a structural advantage in hiring.

Shared infrastructure cost. Open source allows FAANG companies to share the cost of building and maintaining foundational infrastructure. No single company needs to build its own container orchestrator, its own deep learning framework, its own query engine. The cost of infrastructure is amortized across the entire industry.

sections.title: How to Start Contributing to Open Source as an SAP Engineer

description.value:
Most SAP engineers have never submitted a pull request to an open source project. This is not a personal failure — it is a structural consequence of working in a proprietary ecosystem where the concept of open contribution does not exist. Here is a practical path to start contributing:

Start with documentation. Every major open source project has documentation gaps. Fixing a typo, clarifying an unclear paragraph, or adding a missing example is a legitimate contribution that teaches you the project's contribution workflow (fork, branch, commit, PR, review, merge). This is the lowest-friction entry point and builds muscle memory for the open source contribution process.

Fix a bug you encountered. If you use an open source tool at work or in personal projects, you will eventually encounter a bug. Instead of working around it, fix it and submit the fix upstream. This is the most natural contribution path because you already understand the problem and the context.

Add a test. Many open source projects have inadequate test coverage. Adding tests for untested code paths is a high-value contribution that does not require deep domain expertise in the project. Test coverage is universally appreciated by maintainers.

Work on a project you use. The best motivation for contributing to open source is scratching your own itch. If you use Apache Kafka for event streaming, contribute a bug fix or a performance improvement. If you use Prometheus for monitoring, contribute an exporter or an alerting rule.

Your SAP skills are uniquely valuable. Most open source contributors build tools for startups and web applications. Very few contributors build tools for enterprise scenarios — SAP, ERP, mainframe integration, EDI, IDoc processing, SAP PI/PO replacement. This is a gap in the OSS ecosystem where your SAP domain expertise is a competitive advantage. Consider contributing to: Apache Camel (enterprise integration patterns), Apache Flink (stream processing for enterprise data), Debezium (CDC for enterprise databases), or Apache Calcite (SQL query optimization). Your enterprise integration experience is not a limitation — it is a superpower in the open source world.

sections.title: Licensing Basics

description.value:
SAP engineers often have no exposure to open source licensing because SAP does not use or contribute to open source in any meaningful way. At FAANG, understanding open source licenses is essential — using third-party code with the wrong license can create legal liability for the entire company. Here are the three licenses you must understand.

MIT License. The most permissive license. Anyone can use, modify, distribute, and sublicense the code with no restrictions. The only requirement is preserving the copyright notice. MIT is the default choice for most FAANG open source projects because it imposes no obligations on downstream users. Google, Meta, and Microsoft all prefer MIT for most projects.

Apache License 2.0. Also permissive, but includes an explicit patent grant. When a contributor submits code under Apache 2.0, they grant all users a license to any patents that would be infringed by using that code. This is critical for FAANG companies that hold large patent portfolios — it prevents contributors from contributing code and later suing users for patent infringement. Apache 2.0 is preferred by companies with significant patent holdings (Google, Amazon, Apple).

GNU General Public License (GPL). A copyleft license. If you use GPL-licensed code in your project, your entire project must also be GPL-licensed (the 'viral' clause). FAANG companies almost never use GPL-licensed code in internal projects because it would force them to open source their proprietary code. GPL is common in Linux ecosystem tools, but largely absent from FAANG infrastructure. Understanding these distinctions is essential for your first code review at a FAANG company — if you import a library without verifying its license compatibility, your PR will be rejected.

sections.codeBlock:
# License Comparison Table
# MIT v2 — Most permissive, no restrictions beyond copyright notice
# Apache 2.0 — Permissive + explicit patent grant
# GPL v3 — Copyleft: derivative works must be GPL
# LGPL v3 — Weak copyleft: linking is OK, modification must be LGPL
# BSD 3-Clause — Permissive, no endorsement clause
# MPL 2.0 — Weak copyleft: file-level, not project-level
#
# Usage at FAANG:
# Internal tools: MIT or Apache 2.0 only
# Client libraries: Apache 2.0 (patent protection)
# Infrastructure: MIT preferred
# NEVER use GPL in internal repositories (legal/compliance risk)
#
# Key test for SAP engineers:
# If you import a GPL library into a FAANG internal service,
# the company's lawyers will require the entire service to be
# open-sourced under GPL. This will never be approved.
# Rule of thumb: MIT and Apache 2.0 are safe. Everything else
# requires legal review.

details: Staff+ Insight: The single biggest open source advantage for SAP engineers: enterprise integration tools. Most OSS contributors build tools for startups and web applications. Few build tools for enterprise scenarios (SAP, ERP, mainframe integration). This means there's a gap in the OSS ecosystem where your SAP experience is uniquely valuable. Consider contributing to: Apache Camel (enterprise integration patterns), Flink (stream processing for enterprise data), or Debezium (CDC for enterprise databases). Your domain expertise is a competitive advantage in OSS, not a limitation.
